title: Need Podman 3.1.1 in Kubic repo
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

Kubic still has the RPMs for Podman 3.0.1 (in https://download.opensuse.org/repositories/devel:/kubic:/libcontainers:/stable/CentOS_7/x86_64/ for example). Would it be possible to update it to at least 3.1.1?

comments.body:
@lsm5 Were we holding off until we got a successful build through Bodhi in Fedora?

@pascapascapascapasca FYI, we have been having some stability issues with 3.1.x, hence the quick 3.1.0 -> 3.1.1 bump, and it's unclear if they're entirely resolved yet; we might be cutting a 3.1.2 next week. I think there's a general hesitance to push builds to Kubic, where there's no real validation of the quality of the package other than it building successfully, until we have tested elsewhere and confirmed things are working.
Note that CentOS 7 will not receive version of Podman beyond 3.0.0 (see https://podman.io/blogs/2021/03/02/podman-support-for-older-distros.html).

> Note that CentOS 7 will not receive version of Podman beyond 3.0.0

Is there any plan for the stability issues in 3.0.x, beyond "change OS" ?

No, this would be up to community.  We are not supporting or providing newer versions of Podman on RHEL7 or Centos7.

Red Hat engineering will continue to provide CVE fixes for the life of RHEL7, but not on the newer versions of Podman.

> @lsm5 Were we holding off until we got a successful build through Bodhi in Fedora?

@mheon will get to it today.

@pascapascapascapasca ah I just now noticed you mentioned CentOS 7. So like the others mentioned already, there won't be any further updates to CentOS 7 from the containers team, unless community members would like to take it up.

In case anyone in the community is willing to own CentOS 7 builds, I'm open to carrying CentOS 7 patches from them in the rpm spec files if that makes maintenance easier. But I can't spend any more of my time on it. 

HTH.

/cc @alitvak69
v3.1.2 built for centos8 and newer, and ubuntu 20.04 and newer. Closing this, guess we can re-open it if anyone in the community is willing to own CentOS 7. Let me know..

Why was the Ubuntu LTS requirement set higher ? Or was it too much administration to build for both 18.04 and 20.04 ?

Both CentOS 7 and Ubuntu 14.04/16.04 are end-of-life (or at least end-of-updates), so I don't think that is unreasonable.
@afbjorklund 18.04 also has really old packages needing way too much gymnastics for podman.

CentOS 7 is supported until 2024.  Given the current situation with CentOS 8 / CentOS stream, I am sure number of people will use it for a while.  @lsm5 is correct of work required to support packaging for multiple OS distros and versions.  At this point we switched to building  our own packages with static binaries distributed with git or building them using nix.  It is not ideal either but seems to be working with Xenial, Bionic, Focal, and CentOS 7.  We are not planning to use CentOS 8 on bare metal hosts and slowly drifting to Ubuntu (or perhaps Debian if something happens to Ubuntu in the long run).

> @afbjorklund 18.04 also has really old packages needing way too much gymnastics for podman.

OK, I guess they can continue to use podman 2.2.1 until they are ready to upgrade to podman 3.1...

* ~https://github.com/kubernetes/minikube/issues/10649~
* https://github.com/kubernetes/minikube/issues/10937

Unfortunately those packages are missing from the available downloads, similar to the static binaries.
But they could rebuild the binaries themselves, and then install them. Or build from source, perhaps.

The majority of the users seem to be on 20.04, which will be available* for another year (until 22.04).


\* https://podman.io/community/meeting/notes/2021-03-02/#podman-packages-on-kubic
Static binaries missing... Oops. That would be me forgetting to upload the artifacts.

> Static binaries missing... Oops. That would be me forgetting to upload the artifacts.

I don't think there has been any nix binaries for the last couple of releases ? Only podman-remote

https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/8788
Correct - we've never offered non-remote artifacts, from what I can remember.
Thanks for the info all. So, if I want the fix for https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/10015, the solutions would be to either build the package from source or move to CentOS 8?

you just removed the podman 3.0 packages from the repo and broke our entire production pipeline. we're on centos 7 and we're not planing to upgrade the entire os which has vendor support until '24 only because the packages _of one package_ disappeared. i mean, the problem is not that there are no updates... **the packages in the centos 7 folder are just gone**.

yech. what a mess. seriously.
@glaszig you can still find podman in the centos 7 extras repo. And, we've always told production users to use distro packages and not depend on kubic.
